facts_summary:
The petitioner 
                                was a teacher at a government primary school. 
                                A departmental proceeding was drawn against him 
                                for misconduct. An inquiry against him was conducted 
                                ex parte and second show cause notice was served 
                                to him without annexing the inquiry report for 
                                which he could not take any defense. The authority 
                                ultimately dismissed the petitioner from service. Being aggrieved, the petitioner filed a departmental 
                                appeal before the Appellate authority, but the 
                                same was not disposed within 2 months as per the 
                                provisions of the Administrative Tribunal Act. 
                                Therefore, he filed administrative tribunal case 
                                before the Administrative Tribunal, Chittagong. 
                                Administrative Tribunal set aside the impugned 
                                order of dismissal. On appeal the decision was 
                                reversed by the Administrative Appellate Tribunal. 
                                The petitioner then filed a leave to appeal before 
                                the Appellate Division of the Supreme Court. The 
                                impugned decision of the Administrative Appellate 
                                Tribunal was set aside by the Appellate Division 
                                on the ground, among others, that the petitioner 
                                was not given opportunity to cross-examine the 
                                witnesses or to produce evidence in his favour 
                                according to Rule 10 of the Government Servants 
                                (Discipline and Appeal) Rules 1985.
statutes_cited:
Md. Syedul Abrar, son of late 
                                Ahmed Hossain Vs. Government of Bangladesh and others (Obaidul Hassan, J) 15 SCOB [2021] AD 102 Key Words: Administrative Tribunal; Administrative 
                                  Appellate Tribunal; Departmental Inquiry Report; 
                                  Natural Justice; Disciplinary proceedings
notes: reversed

facts_summary:
The respondents 
                                are work-charged employees under different government 
                                departments who filed different Writ Petitions 
                                in the High Court Division and obtained directions 
                                upon the writ respondents-petitioners to regularize/absorb 
                                their service in the revenue set up. The Government 
                                and others preferred different Civil Petitions 
                                for Leave to Appeal which were dismissed as being 
                                time barred. Thereafter, the government and others 
                                filed these review petitions. Disposing of all 
                                the review petitions the Appellate Division observed 
                                that the service rendered by work-charged employees 
                                for a considerable period, like 20 years or more, 
                                may be considered to be permanent employees and 
                                they may be qualified for grant of pensionary 
                                benefit. Citing different measures taken by the 
                                different State Governments of India for work-charged 
                                employees, the Appellate Division further observed 
                                that the Government should formulate a policy 
                                instrument for giving pensionary and other benefits 
                                to the work-charged employees who have served 
                                without break for a considerable period of time 
                                i.e for 20 years or more.
notes: manual

facts_summary:
Question arose 
                                in this case as to whether the petitioner-respondent 
                                who left for Japan for higher training with the 
                                leave of the Government for 6 months and availed 
                                a further leave of another 3 (three) months as 
                                leave outside Bangladesh and joined in his post 
                                after 7 years 7 months 24 days being absent from 
                                service during this time without any leave from 
                                the competent authority, have ceased to be a government 
                                servant in accordance with Rule 34, 1st Part of 
                                the Bangladesh Service Rules, in spite of the 
                                fact that he was initially permitted to rejoin 
                                in the post and worked there for about 1 year 
                                and 7 months. The Administrative Appellate Tribunal 
                                decided that by accepting the joining of the respondent-petitioner 
                                the Director General of Industry and Labour Wing 
                                retrospectively approved his unauthorized leave 
                                and the Government waived its right to reject 
                                the rejoining of the petitioner in service. The 
                                Appellate Division held that the Director General 
                                was not empowered to act under rule 34 and therefore, 
                                his act of allowing the respondent rejoining in 
                                service was not only without lawful authority 
                                but also void ab intio. The Court also held that 
                                the doctrine of estoppels, waiver and acquiescence 
                                is not applicable against statutory provisions 
                                and as such, though the respondent has served 
                                for about 1 year and 7 months after rejoining 
                                in the service, that cannot be deemed to be a 
                                waiver by the government against the clear statutory 
                                provision embodied in rule 34.
statutes_cited:
Bangladesh 
                                and another Vs. Sayed Mahabubul Karim ( Md. Nuruzzaman, J) 16 SCOB [2022] AD 46 Key Words: Rule 34, 1st Part of the Bangladesh Service 
                                  Rules; Doctrine of estoppels, waiver and acquiescence; 
                                  void ab intio
notes: manual

facts_summary:
The question 
                                came up for consideration in this case whether 
                                after transfer by pre-emptee-opposite party no.1 
                                to co-sharer opposite party no.6 the pre-emptory 
                                right of the pre-emptor exists or not. The Appellate 
                                Division examining section 90 and 96 of State 
                                Acquisition and Tenancy Act and the view taken 
                                by their lordship in the case of 50 C.W.N. 806 
                                as well as 35 DLR 238 and also distinguishing 
                                the facts of 35 DLR (AD) 225 held that even after 
                                subsequent transfer by the stranger pre-emptee 
                                to another co-sharer of the holding, the pre-emptory 
                                right of a co-sharer pre-emptor will not be defeated.
notes: manual

facts_summary:
In this case 
                                the Government made a delay of 403 days in filing 
                                a revisional application before the High Court 
                                Division against the judgment and decree of the 
                                Appellate Court in which a bil (water body) recorded 
                                in Khas Khatian was decreed in favour of the respondents. 
                                The High Court Division, however, refused to condone 
                                the delay and discharged the Rule. The Government 
                                preferred this petition against the judgment and 
                                order of the High Court Division. Appellate Division 
                                held that the delay was made due to exhaustion 
                                of the official formalities which was beyond the 
                                control of the Government and it was not an inordinate 
                                delay which could not be condoned. Consequently, 
                                the Appellate Division set aside the judgment 
                                and order of the High Court Division and condoned 
                                the delay made by the Government.
statutes_cited:
Govt. 
                                of Bangladesh & ors Vs. Md. Abdul Jalil & ors (Md. Abu Zafor Siddique, 
                                  J) 17 SCOB [2023] AD 74 Key Words: Section 5 of Limitation Act, 1908; Condonation 
                                  of delay; delay made by the government; Section 
                                  115(1) of the Code of Civil Procedure
notes: manual

facts_summary:
A retired public 
                                servant filed a writ petition in relation to his 
                                service matter and got a rule and stay in his 
                                favour. The Government filed leave petition in 
                                the Appellate Division against the interim order 
                                of the High Court Division challenging its legality 
                                arguing that in service matter even retired public 
                                servants are required to seek relief in the Administrative 
                                Tribunal in view of section 4(3) of the Administrative 
                                Tribunal Act, 1980. Appellate Division accepted 
                                the argument of the Government and found that 
                                in an earlier decision reported in 71 DLR (AD) 
                                319 the highest court wrongly held that in service 
                                matter writ petition by retired public servant 
                                is maintainable. The Appellate Division then departed 
                                from its earlier decision finding it to be per 
                                incuriam and discharged the Rule issued by the 
                                High Court Division. However, the Court also observed 
                                that in view of the article 111 of the Constitution, 
                                High Court Division is not competent to hold any 
                                decision of the Appellate Division to be per incuriam 
                                and it must follow the decision in toto. High 
                                Court Division only can bring the matter in the 
                                notice of the Honorable Chief Justice of Bangladesh. 
                                Similarly, subordinate Courts have no jurisdiction 
                                to raise any question regarding the legality of 
                                the judgment of the High Court Division saying 
                                that it was a judgment per incuriam. Because only 
                                a Court equivalent to the Court which pronounced 
                                the judgment per incuriam is free to depart from 
                                a decision of that Court where the earlier judgment 
                                was decided wrongly.
statutes_cited:
Secretary, 
                                Posts & Telecom Div. & anr Vs. Shudangshu Shekhar & ors (Obaidul Hassan, 
                                  J) 18 SCOB [2023] AD 11 Key Words: Per incuriam; Section 4(3) of the Administrative 
                                  Tribunal Act, 1980; Article 111 of the Constitution; 
                                  maintainability of the writ petition by a retired 
                                  public servant in service matter
notes: manual

facts_summary:
The suit property 
                                belonged to Rukkhini Dashi who purchased the same 
                                from her Stridhan fund. Rukkhini Dashi died leaving 
                                only daughter Hazari Sundory Dashi who also died 
                                leaving only daughter the plaintiff Elokeshi Mondol. 
                                Defendant nos.1-6 who were paternal uncles of 
                                the plaintiff, managed to get the suit land recorded 
                                in their names in the S.A. record. When the defendants 
                                denied the title of the plaintiff, she filed the 
                                present suit. The trial Court decreed the suit 
                                in favor of the plaintiff. The Appellate Court 
                                confirmed it and on revision the High Court Division 
                                affirmed the judgments and decrees of the Courts 
                                bellow. The concurrent findings of the Courts 
                                were that the suit property was the Stridhan property 
                                of Rukkhini Dashi. The defendants filed appeal 
                                before the Appellate Division contending that 
                                according to the ‘Dayabhaga’ school, property 
                                inherited by a woman whether from a male or from 
                                a female, does not become her Stridhan and she 
                                takes only a limited interest in the property 
                                and on her death the property passes not to her 
                                heirs but to next heir of the person from whom 
                                she inherited it and if the property is inherited 
                                from a female, it will pass to the next Stridhan 
                                heirs of such female, thus the impugned judgment 
                                and order is liable to be set-aside. On the contrary, 
                                the contention of the plaintiff-respondents were- 
                                when a daughter inherits Stridhan of her mother, 
                                she takes it absolutely like a son because son 
                                and daughter inherit equally and she acquires 
                                all the rights to dispose of the Stridhana property 
                                at her will and there is no express text restricting 
                                women’s heritable right inasmuch as equality is 
                                the Rule where no distinction is expressed and 
                                as such Elukeshi Mondol is entitled to get the 
                                property of her grandmother Rukkhini Dashi after 
                                the death of her mother Hazari Sundory Dashi. 
                                The Appellate Division, however, examining the 
                                texts from ‘The Dayabhaga’ by Jimuta Vahana, Mulla’s 
                                principle of Hindu Law and hearing opinion of 
                                the Amicus Curiae accepted the argument of the 
                                plaintiff-respondents and dismissed the civil 
                                appeal with some observations.
statutes_cited:
Mrigangka 
                                Mohan Dhali & ors Vs. Chitta Ranjan Mondol & ors (Borhanuddin, 
                                  J) 18 SCOB [2023] AD 20 Key Words: The Hindu Law of Inheritance (Amendment) Act, 
                                  1929; Dayabhaga Law of Inheritance; Stridhan; 
                                  doctrine of religious efficacy; limited interest
notes: affirmed

facts_summary:
In the appointment 
                                letter of the writ petitioner it was clearly mentioned 
                                that her appointment as a Junior Officer was on 
                                a temporary basis without mentioning in it any 
                                period for which she was appointed. She was assigned 
                                various duties by the authority during her service 
                                which indicated her good performance and she received 
                                a pay rise. Suddenly, the authority issued a show 
                                cause notice as to why she would not be removed 
                                from service for dissatisfactory performance requiring 
                                her to make the reply within one week. The writ-petitioner 
                                replied describing her good performance during 
                                her service but paying no heed to the reply and 
                                without giving any opportunity of personal hearing 
                                she was removed from service. The High Court Division 
                                directed the writ respondent to reinstate the 
                                writ petitioner. On appeal, the Appellate Division 
                                found that the writ petitioner could not be termed 
                                as temporary appointee because no specific period 
                                of her appointment was mentioned in the appointment 
                                letter. The Court also held that principle of 
                                natural justice demands before putting stigma 
                                of inefficiency an opportunity of being heard 
                                should have been given to the writ-petitioner. 
                                Mere mentioning of inefficiency in the impugned 
                                order of removal is nothing but an arbitrariness 
                                on the part of the authority. Consequently, the 
                                appeal was dismissed.
notes: manual

facts_summary:
In these appeals 
                                and civil petitions the issues involved for determination, 
                                among others, were whether the claim of the BTRC 
                                for additional spectrum fee based on Market Competition 
                                Factor (MCF) is lawful; whether the Cellular Mobile 
                                Phone Operator can withhold the VAT collected 
                                at source and then pay the same directly to Government 
                                exchequer and VAT paid by the Cellular Mobile 
                                Phone Operator is rebatable or not; and whether 
                                BTRC requires a compulsory registration under 
                                the VAT Act. Contrary to some findings of the 
                                High Court Division, the Appellate Division analyzing 
                                relevant laws, rules and guidelines held that 
                                (1) in accordance with clause-12(viii) of the 
                                Cellular Mobile Phone Operator Regulatory and 
                                Licensing Guidelines, 2011 BTRC reserves the right 
                                to make any change in the charges or levies from 
                                time to time and the mobile phone operators are 
                                bound to abide by such decision and as such, additional 
                                spectrum fee based on Market Competition Factor 
                                (MCF) is lawful; (2) the Cellular Mobile Phone 
                                Operator cannot withhold the VAT collected at 
                                source, the BTRC is to collect VAT from the Cellular 
                                Mobile Phone Operators and then deposit it to 
                                the Government exchequer and VAT paid by the cellular 
                                mobile companies on the spectrum fees and the 
                                license fees are not rebatable; and (3) under 
                                clause-7 (Ab¨vb¨ †mev)(N) of the second schedule 
                                of the VAT Act, 1991 compulsory VAT registration 
                                is not necessary for BTRC.
statutes_cited:
Grameenphone 
                                Ltd & ors Vs. BTRC & Ors (Borhanuddin, J) 19 SCOB [2024] AD 96 Key Words: Sub-Section 3(Ga) of Section 3, 5(4), Sub-Section 
                                  4 of Section 6, Section 6 (4KaKa), Section 9(Uma), 
                                  15 (4) of the VAT Act, 1991; Rule 18(Ka) and 
                                  18(Uma) of the VAT Rules, 1991; Cellular Mobile 
                                  Phone Operator Regulatory and Licensing Guidelines, 
                                  2011; Market Competition Factor (MCF);
notes: manual

facts_summary:
In this case 
                                Hakkani Publishers moved the High Court Division 
                                claiming that the Ministry of Information issued 
                                work order to supply 2317 sets of the h¡wm¡­c­nl

                                Appellate Division found that different correspondences 
                                took place between and among different ministries

facts_summary:
The appellant 
                                along with respondent No.2, as plaintiffs instituted 
                                Title Suit No. 199 of 2001 before the 1st Court 
                                of Assistant Judge, Dhaka, praying for declaration 
                                that the application of Conservancy and Pilotage 
                                Service Fees Rules, 1990 over the Coastal Ships 
                                and Tankers of the plaintiffs established under 
                                the Merchants Shipping Ordinance, 1983 is illegal 
                                and also for declaration that the letters dated 
                                17.06.2001 and 25.6.2001 and circular vide letter 
                                No. M- 2/15/47(4) are illegal and unlawful. The trial Court dismissed the suit. Against which 
                                appeal was preferred and the same was allowed 
                                by setting aside the judgment and decree of the 
                                trial Court. Challenging the said decision the 
                                defendants filed Civil Revision before the High 
                                Court Division and obtained Rule. A Single Bench 
                                of the High Court Division made the Rule absolute 
                                upon setting aside the judgment and decree of 
                                the appellate Court and affirming those of the 
                                trial Court. Hence, the plaintiff No. 1 preferred 
                                civil petition for leave to appeal before the 
                                Appellate Division and obtained leave giving rise 
                                to this appeal which was dismissed accordingly.
statutes_cited:
Bangladesh 
                                  TailbahiJahaj Malik Samity Vs. Chairman, 
                                  BIWTA (Md. Ashfaqul Islam, J) 20 SCOB [2025] AD Key Words: নৌসংরক্ষণওপরিচালনএবংপথনির্দেশনাফিসবিধিমালা, 
                                  ১৯৯০; Merchant Shipping Ordinance, 1983; Definition of Inland Ship; Imposition of Conservancy fees upon Inland Ship 
                                  according to Bidhimala,1990; Double Jeopardy;
notes: rule absolute

facts_summary:
The appellants 
                                successfully passed the viva voce examination, 
                                with the final result being published on 21.01.2007. 
                                The Bangladesh Public Service Commission recommended 
                                3567 successful candidates, including the appellants, 
                                for being appointed in different cadres. In the 
                                meantime, some reports were published in different 
                                newspapers raising certain allegations with regard 
                                to the 27th BCS examination, which was, however, 
                                refuted by BPSC. Later, in an unprecedented move, the then Caretaker 
                                Government decided to cancel the final result 
                                of the 27th BCS which had already been published 
                                by BPSC. Accordingly, the Ministry of Establishment 
                                requested BPSC to retake the viva-voce examination 
                                of all the successful candidates of the 27th BCS 
                                upon cancelling the result of the previous viva 
                                voce examination. Meanwhile, the second viva voce examination was 
                                conducted and the result was published on 23.09.2008, 
                                recommending 3239 candidates for being appointed 
                                in different cadres under the 27th BCS, excluding, 
                                however, 1137 candidates including the appellants, 
                                who had previously qualified in the first viva 
                                voce examination. Accordingly, a notification 
                                was published in the Bangladesh Gazette on 10.11.2008. The present appellants along with several other 
                                candidates, who had earlier qualified in the first 
                                viva voce examination, filed Writ Petitionschallenging 
                                the result published by BPSC on 23.09.2008 as 
                                well as the office order dated 20.10.2008. Upon 
                                hearing the parties, the High Court Division disposed 
                                of the Rules by judgments and orders dated 11.11.2009 
                                and 26.01.2010, directing the concerned respondents 
                                to appoint the candidates, including the present 
                                appellants, who had been successful in the first 
                                viva voce examination, in their respective cadres. Challenging the said decision, the Government 
                                preferred Civil Petition for Leave to Appeal Nos.512, 
                                513 and 514 of 2010. By judgment and order dated 
                                11.07.2010, this Division disposed of the leave 
                                petitions upon setting aside the judgment and 
                                order of the High Court Division dated 11.11.2009. 
                                Being aggrieved thereby, the present appellants 
                                preferred Civil Review Petition Nos.197-199 of 
                                2024 and leave was granted by this Division by 
                                order dated 07.11.2024, which gave rise to the 
                                present Civil Appeals which were allowed by this 
                                judgment.
statutes_cited:
Mohammad 
                                  Sohel Rana & others Vs. Bangladesh 
                                  Public Service Commission (Zubayer Rahman Chowdhury, J) 20 SCOB [2025] AD Key Words: Article 31,102 of the Constitution of Bangladesh; Setting aside judgment of the High Court Division 
                                  without granting leave; Principle of natural justice, audi alteram partem– 
                                  no man should be condemned unheard; Justice hurried, Justice buried;
notes: manual

facts_summary:
Four civil appeals 
                                (Nos. 171–174 of 2015) arose from a High Court 
                                Division judgment (13.02.2014) that declared certain 
                                land acquisition proceedings in Chittagong unlawful. 
                                The disputes centered around Land Acquisition 
                                Case No.1/2010-2011, where the Ministry of Land 
                                approved acquisition of lands for a power project 
                                without the alleged mandatory approval of the 
                                Prime Minister. The writ petitioners (various 
                                companies and individuals) claimed they had valid 
                                leases or ownership and that their objections 
                                to acquisition were ignored. They relied on a 
                                note dated 14.11.2010, allegedly signed by the 
                                then Prime Minister, directing the Ministry to 
                                submit a fresh report, which was never complied 
                                with. The High Court held that the Ministry’s 
                                memo dated 23.11.2010 was issued without lawful 
                                authority, malafide, and unconstitutional, since 
                                the Prime Minister’s approval was required. The 
                                Deputy Commissioner, Chittagong, appealed, arguing 
                                that the Prime Minister’s note was fictitious 
                                and that the High Court had relied on forged documents. 
                                The Appellate Division found substance in this 
                                claim, noting that the writ petitioners had quoted 
                                from confidential government files without authority. 
                                The Court emphasized that fraud vitiates all legal 
                                proceedings and that no relief can be granted 
                                to litigants who approach the court with “unclean 
                                hands.” It held that the alleged note of 14.11.2010 
                                was non-existent and fictitious, thereby invalidating 
                                the foundation of the High Court’s decision. Consequently, 
                                the Appellate Division struck down the High Court’s 
                                judgment and allowed all four civil appeals, restoring 
                                the government’s acquisition process.
statutes_cited:
Deputy 
                                  Commissioner, Chittagong Vs. Interbulk 
                                  Overseas SA Bangladesh Limited and another. (Farah Mahbub, J) 20 SCOB [2025] AD Key Words: Settlement case, The Acquisition and Requisition 
                                  of Immovable Property Ordinance, 1982, L.A. 
                                  Case, Lease agreement, clean hands, acquisition 
                                  process, fraud upon the Court,
notes: declared

facts_summary:
The petitioner 
                                approached the Company Court By invoking Section 
                                43 of the Companies Act, 1994 for rectification 
                                of the Members’ Register of the Chittagong Club 
                                Ltd, a private company limited by guarantee without 
                                having any share capital incorporated under the 
                                Companies Act towards restoration of the petitioner’s 
                                name therein, through obtaining a declaration 
                                from the Court that the decision of the General 
                                Committee (GC) so far as it relates to suspension 
                                of the membership of the petitioner is illegal 
                                and not binding upon him. The High Court Division 
                                after elaborate discussion of the relevant provisions 
                                of the Companies Act, 1994 and rules framed under 
                                it, dismissed the petition on the ground of maintainability 
                                and held that the dispute being purely of civil 
                                nature, the petitioner’s remedy lies in the civil 
                                Court. The Company court also imposed a cost of 
                                100,000 taka upon the petitioner for wasting court’s 
                                valuable time by pressing such meritless case 
                                before it.
statutes_cited:
Engr. Md. Anwar Hossen -VERSUS- Chittagong Club Ltd and others 15SCOB [2021] HCD 60 (Muhammad Khurshid Alam Sarkar, J) Key Words: Golden rule of statutory interpretation; Section 
                                  43 and 44 of Companies Act, 1994; Rule 8 of 
                                  the Companies Rules, 2009; Section 2(1)(d), 
                                  Section 3(1), Section 43 and Section 233 of 
                                  the Companies Act, 1994
notes: rejected

facts_summary:
The questions 
                                arose in this case are (1) what is the time limit 
                                for preferring appeal under Section 100(2) of 
                                the Trade Mark Act, 2009 from the order or decision 
                                of the Registrar of the Department of Patents, 
                                Designs and Trade Marks and (2) whether section 
                                5 of the Limitation Act, 1908 is applicable for 
                                condonation of delay in preferring appeal under 
                                the said section of the Act. Analyzing different 
                                sections of Trade Mark Act 2009 and relevant Rules 
                                of Trade Mark Rules, 2025 the High Court Division 
                                came to the conclusion that time period for preferring 
                                appeal under section 100(2) is 2(two) months and 
                                time starts from the date of receipt of the certified 
                                copy of the order or decision of the Registrar. 
                                The Court also held that Trade Mark Act, 2009 
                                being a special law section 5 of the Limitation 
                                Act, 1908 cannot be applied for condoning delay 
                                in preferring appeal under section 100(2) of the 
                                Mark Act, 2009.
statutes_cited:
Kazi 
                                Md. Kamrul Islam Vs. Registrar, Dep. of PDTM & ors (Farah Mahbub, J) 18 SCOB [2023] HCD 1 Key Words: Section 5, 29(2) and Article 156 of the 1st 
                                  Schedule of the Limitation Act, 1908; Section 
                                  2(12), 100 of the Trade Mark Act, 2009; Supreme 
                                  Court of Bangladesh (High Court Division) Rules, 
                                  1973; Article 107(1) of the Constitution of 
                                  the People’s Republic of Bangladesh; Order XLI 
                                  Rule 1, Order XLIII Rule 2 of the Code of Civil 
                                  Procedure; Rule 10, 14, 15 and 50(1) of Trade 
                                  Mark Rules, 2015;
notes: manual

facts_summary:
In this case 
                                petitioner challenged an order passed by the learned 
                                District Judge, Dhaka in an Arbitration Miscellaneous 
                                Case whereby the said court rejected an application 
                                for calling for the record of arbitration proceedings 
                                from the arbitrators. Question arose as to whether 
                                a civil revision is maintainable against any interlocutory 
                                order passed in an application under section 42 
                                of the Arbitration Act, 2001. The honorable Chief 
                                Justice constituted a Special Bench under Rule 
                                1C of Chapter 2 of the Supreme Court of Bangladesh 
                                (High Court Division) Rules 1973 to decide the 
                                matter. The Court, after discussing different 
                                provisions of the Arbitration Act 2001, General 
                                Clauses Act 1897 and relevant case laws, observed 
                                that the term “‡RjvRR Av`vjZ” as mentioned in 
                                the Arbitration Act, 2001 means the ‘Court of 
                                District Judge’, not ‘persona designata’ and any 
                                decision passed in a proceeding under this Act 
                                is amenable in a civil revision under the Code 
                                of Civil Procedure and as such, the civil revision 
                                is maintainable. Nevertheless, the Court discharged 
                                the rule rejecting the civil revision contending 
                                that since the petitioner had an arbitrator appointed 
                                by him, he could have easily obtained a copy of 
                                the proceeding from his arbitrator. This application 
                                for calling for records is unnecessary and only 
                                to cause delay. The Court further observed that 
                                the Government should frame necessary rules regarding 
                                how long and under what modes the arbitrators 
                                will maintain the record of any arbitration proceedings 
                                after giving the arbitral award.
statutes_cited:
Mitul 
                                Properties Ltd Vs. M.N.H. Bulu (Khizir Ahmed Choudhury, J) 18 SCOB [2023] HCD 257 Key Words: Persona Designata; Maintainability of Civil 
                                  Revision; Arbitral Award; Supervisory Jurisdiction 
                                  of the High Court Division; The Arbitration 
                                  Act, 2001
notes: no merit

facts_summary:
In this case 
                                the petitioner Danish Food Limited filed an application 
                                under Section 42 and 51 of the Trade Marks Act, 
                                2009 for removal of trade mark consisting of the 
                                word ‘RANI’ granted by the respondent No.2 in 
                                favour of the respondent No.1 from the register. 
                                High Court Division, however, hearing both sides, 
                                found that section 51 has no manner of application 
                                in this case and stipulations provided in section 
                                42 of the Act, have not been fulfilled so as to 
                                order the removal. Therefore, it rejected the 
                                application.
notes: rejected

facts_summary:
The petitioner-judgment 
                                debtor filed an application for dismissal of an 
                                execution case as being time barred. The learned 
                                Senior Assistant Judge rejected the application 
                                relying on a synopsis of a decision of one of 
                                the High Courts of Pakistan passed in 1998 published 
                                in a D.L.R. reference book which was affirmed 
                                by the High Court Division. The Appellate Division, 
                                however, found that the decision of the High Court 
                                of Pakistan is not applicable in our jurisdiction 
                                after 25th March 1971 and detailed as to which 
                                precedents of Dhaka High Court, Federal Court 
                                of Pakistan, Supreme Court of Pakistan, Calcutta 
                                High Court, Federal Court of India and the Privy 
                                Council are binding on us and which are not. Finally, 
                                finding that the execution proceeding was initiated 
                                after 3 years beyond the permissible period under 
                                Article 182 of the Limitation Act, dismissed the 
                                execution case.
statutes_cited:
Terab 
                                Ali & ors Vs. Syed Ullah & ors ( Md. Nuruzzaman, J) 17 SCOB [2023] AD 34 Key Words: Persuasive efficacy; interpretation of law; 
                                  Article 111 read with Article 149 of the Constitution 
                                  of Bangladesh, 1972
notes: affirmed

facts_summary:
In this case 
                                the finding of the Administrative Appellate Tribunal 
                                that the impugned penalty imposed on the respondent 
                                was illegal as because that was imposed by the 
                                Director General who was, admittedly, holding 
                                current charge and was not the appointing authority, 
                                was challenged. Appellate Division analyzing the 
                                relevant provisions of law came to the conclusion 
                                that when current charge was given for unlimited 
                                period it was to be presumed that he had been 
                                given all the administrative and financial power 
                                of the institution. Consequently, Appellate Division 
                                set aside the judgment and order of the Administrative 
                                Appellate Tribunal.
statutes_cited:
Bangladesh 
                                & anr Vs. Nazrul Islam Biswas (M. Enayetur Rahim, J) 19 SCOB [2024] AD 119 Key Words: Administrative Tribunal; Administrative Appellate 
                                  Tribunal; Current Charge; Section 4 (3) (b) 
                                  of the Government Servants (Discipline and Appeal) 
                                  Rules, 1985
notes: manual

facts_summary:
Senior Assistant 
                                Secretary, Ministry of Rural Development and Co-operative 
                                issued a letter on 21.03.2005 selecting site of 
                                Pashapol Union Parishad Complex Building at Pashapol. 
                                Theplaintiffs filed the present case arguing that 
                                the government earlier recognized the old site 
                                for Pashapol Union Parishad Office at Bariali 
                                Mouza where all the requirements for construction 
                                of the Complex Building were available. Ignoring 
                                that site the government's letter dated 21.03.2005 
                                is illegal. The defendants denied the facts of 
                                the plaintiffs and stated inter-alia that Pashapol 
                                is the centre point of the Union. There are hut, 
                                Educational Institutions, mosque and others. They 
                                prayed for dismissal of the suit.The trial Court 
                                dismissed the suit. The Appellate Court allowed 
                                the appeal, reversed the judgment of the trial 
                                court and decreed the suit in favor of the plaintiffs.On 
                                revision the High Court Division affirmed the 
                                judgment of the Appellate Court. The defendants 
                                preferred leave to appeal before the Appellate 
                                Division while the leave was granted. The petitioners 
                                submit that the selection of site of Union Parishad 
                                is the business of the government and relates 
                                to policy matters and as such the plaintiffs have 
                                no locus standi to challenge such policy decision 
                                of the government. On the contrary, the contention 
                                of the respondents are- specific guidelines were 
                                given by circular dated 28.12.2005 issued by the 
                                Ministry of Local Government Rural Development 
                                and Co-operative vide its memo no.ইপ/অঙন/০৬/২০০৫/১৬৮০(৫০১) 
                                in respect of selection of site by government. 
                                Those guidelines have not been followed in the 
                                impugned letter. The Appellate Division holds 
                                the view that shifting of site of the Union Parishad 
                                is a policy matter of the government and administrative 
                                decision. The plaintiffs have no local standi 
                                to challenge the policy making power of the government 
                                and as such the appeal is allowed and suit is 
                                dismissed.
statutes_cited:
Md. 
                                  Obaidul Islam and others Vs. Md.Abdul 
                                  Mazid Mondal and others, (A.K.M. Asaduzzaman, J) 20 SCOB [2025] AD Key Words: Declaration; selection of site of Union Parishad; 
                                  representative character suit; policy decision 
                                  of government; administrative decision
notes: appeal is allowed

facts_summary: ASK filed public interest litigation challenging eviction notices issued to slum residents on government land, arguing violation of fundamental rights. Government contended the land was allotted for government office construction and petitioners were unauthorized occupants without legal right to shelter.
statutes_cited: Government and Local Authority Lands and Buildings (Recovery of Possession) Ordinance 1970 Section 5(1); Constitution Articles 27, 31, 32
notes: Rule discharged; right to shelter is non-justiciable and unlawful occupation creates no legitimate expectation against eviction

facts_summary: Plaintiffs sought declaration of title over land lost to river erosion in 1967 that reappeared in 1974. Government argued land vested in the Government under Section 86 of the State Acquisition and Tenancy Act as amended in 1972 since it reappeared after the cut-off date.
statutes_cited: State Acquisition and Tenancy Act 1950 Section 86; P.O. No. 135 of 1972
notes: Appeal dismissed; land reappearing after 1972 vests absolutely in Government; plaintiffs' rights were extinguished

facts_summary: Petitioners challenged a District Judge's order in a title execution case that condoned delay and admitted revision but refrained from granting ad-interim stay of execution proceedings. Petitioners argued non-grant of stay amounted to implied refusal.
statutes_cited: Code of Civil Procedure Section 115(4)
notes: Rule made absolute; non-grant of interim stay constitutes implied refusal entitling petitioners to seek revisional remedy

facts_summary: Plaintiff claimed right to repurchase property based on a reconveyance deed executed in 1963 with a repurchase condition valid until 1971. P.O. No. 88 of 1972 came into effect after the repurchase condition had already expired, making the transaction past and closed.
statutes_cited: State Acquisition and Tenancy Act 1950 Sections 95 and 95A; P.O. No. 88 of 1972; P.O. No. 136 of 1972
notes: Plaintiff not entitled to relief as repurchase condition expired before Presidential Order; transaction was past and closed

facts_summary: Plaintiff filed partition suit claiming share in jointly held land. Defendants challenged plaintiff's title. Court held that when defendants question title in a partition suit the court must assess and determine the plaintiff's title before ordering partition.
statutes_cited: Code of Civil Procedure Section 54; Order 20 Rule 18; Order 26 Rule 13
notes: Remanded for proper adjudication of title and partition together to avoid multiplicity of proceedings

appellant: East West Property Development (Pvt.) Ltd. and another
facts_summary: Petitioner challenged cancellation of property mutation by Deputy Commissioner based on an acquisition proceeding that had become non-est due to repeal of the relevant law. No government decision for valid acquisition of the property had been issued.
statutes_cited: Emergency Requisition of Property Act 1948; স্থাবর সম্পত্তি অধিগ্রহণ ও হুকুমদখল আইন ২০১৭
notes: Property acquisition found invalid; no government decision existed and acquisition proceeding became non-est due to statutory repeal

appellant: Monto Sheikh being dead his legal heirs
respondent: Ibrahim Miah being dead his legal heirs
facts_summary: Plaintiff claimed title and possession over suit land but could not prove title. The basis of the plaintiff's claim was a settlement that had been cancelled with the cancellation order still in existence.
statutes_cited: Evidence Act 1872; Code of Civil Procedure
notes: Plaintiff failed to prove title; cancelled settlement cannot form valid basis of title claim

respondent: Charge Officer and Appeal Officer and others
facts_summary: Petitioners challenged a Settlement Officer who exercised power under Rule 42 to nullify land record proceedings up to the Appellate Division level, reversing the entire course of the parent law's prescribed sequence. Government had not delegated power to reverse parent law proceedings.
statutes_cited: State Acquisition and Tenancy Rules 1955 Rule 42; Settlement Officer jurisdiction
notes: Settlement Officer's nullification of parent law proceedings declared a nullity; revenue officers cannot reverse what Parliament prescribed

facts_summary: Petitioners challenged cancellation of their land lease where the authority cancelled the lease first and then called them to appear with valid papers afterward. The principle of natural justice required hearing before decision, not after.
statutes_cited: Principles of natural justice; audi alteram partem
notes: Authority violated natural justice by cancelling lease before affording petitioners opportunity to be heard; hearing must be pre-decisional

facts_summary: Pre-emption case where the pre-emptee claimed protection under Section 96(10) of the State Acquisition and Tenancy Act as a relation within three degrees. Court examined whether a brother-in-law qualified within three degrees of consanguinity under the Succession Act.
statutes_cited: State Acquisition and Tenancy Act 1950 Section 96(10); Succession Act 1925 Section 28
notes: Pre-emptee not entitled to protection as brother-in-law is not within three degrees of consanguinity; pre-emption protection denied

facts_summary: Plaintiff landlord sought ejectment of defendants who denied the plaintiff's title to the land. Court examined whether notice under Section 106 of the Transfer of Property Act was required when defendants deny the landlord's title.
statutes_cited: Transfer of Property Act Section 106
notes: Defendants forfeited right to stay by denying landlord's title; notice under Section 106 dispensed with when tenant denies title

facts_summary: Plaintiff claimed title over land that diluvated before 1965 and was recorded as government khas land. Government argued land vested under Section 86 of SAT Act. Court examined legal effect of diluvion and government's right to lease reappeared land.
statutes_cited: State Acquisition and Tenancy Act 1950 Section 86; P.O. No. 135 of 1972
notes: Government acquired lawful right to lease diluvated land; plaintiff's title extinguished after diluvion regardless of prior ownership

facts_summary: Plaintiff sought specific performance of a 1978 agreement to purchase land where he paid Tk. 18,000 earnest money but vendor refused to execute sale deed. Defendants claimed independent title through an earlier registered gift deed from 1972 and alleged the agreement was forged.
statutes_cited: Specific Relief Act Section 25(a); Code of Civil Procedure
notes: Case remanded for expert comparison of thumb impressions and determination of third-party title and possession; plaintiff gets proper adjudication

appellant: Md. Badaruddin being dead his heirs
facts_summary: Plaintiff sought specific performance of a Bainapatra contract for sale of property. Defendant argued time in arbitration proceedings should extend the limitation period. Court held equity aids the vigilant and specific performance requires prompt filing.
statutes_cited: Limitation Act 1908 Article 113; Specific Relief Act
notes: Time in arbitration did not extend limitation; specific performance denied as plaintiff did not come promptly to court

respondent: Central Procurement Technical Unit (CPTU) and others
facts_summary: Petitioner contractor challenged a Review Panel decision that dismissed their procurement tender appeal without properly considering its merits. The legal question was whether CPTU Review Panel had jurisdiction to allow an appeal or only dismiss it.
statutes_cited: Public Procurement Rules; CPTU Review Panel jurisdiction
notes: Review Panel has jurisdiction to allow appeals as well as dismiss them; dismissal without proper consideration was improper

facts_summary: Petitioner challenged Review Panel decision in a procurement tender case where the Panel exceeded its jurisdiction under PPR Rule 60 and the authority refused to issue a No Objection Certificate despite the petitioner winning the tender.
statutes_cited: Public Procurement Rules 2008 Rule 60; CPTU jurisdiction
notes: Review Panel exceeded jurisdiction; failure to issue NOC found manifestly arbitrary without lawful authority; petitioner entitled to NOC

appellant: Shetu International Pvt. Ltd and others
respondent: Artha Rin Adalat-2, Dhaka and another
facts_summary: Petitioner challenged an Artha Rin court order where the bank published summons in newspaper without a court order and without first attempting service through process server or post, contrary to mandatory procedure under Section 7(1) of the Artha Rin Adalat Ain 2003.
statutes_cited: Artha Rin Adalat Ain 2003 Sections 6(2) and 7(1)
notes: Newspaper publication without prior court order found contrary to mandatory procedural requirements; bank cannot take unilateral steps

facts_summary: Petitioner participated in a government tender for land, paid earnest money as accepted by the authority, but the government attempted to change the valuation and terms after the tender was accepted. Court examined basic principles of offer and acceptance in contract law.
statutes_cited: Contract Act 1872; principles of offer and acceptance; legitimate expectation
notes: Government bound by tender offer once accepted with payment; sale binding from moment tender conditions complied with

facts_summary: Artha Rin suit where the borrower sought court intervention to exempt him from paying high interest on a bank loan. Court examined whether Artha Rin courts had power to exempt defendants from interest liability under the Artha Rin Adalat Ain.
statutes_cited: Artha Rin Adalat Ain 2003 Section 50
notes: Court has no power to exempt defendant from interest liability; only the financial institution itself can waive interest on its loans

appellant: Jafri Soap and Chemical Industries and others
facts_summary: Petitioner sought remedy outside the Artha Rin Adalat Ain 2003 framework challenging orders in Artha Rin proceedings. Court examined whether parties could bypass the mandatory provisions of the Ain.
statutes_cited: Artha Rin Adalat Ain 2003 Sections 19, 20, 41, 42
notes: No court can entertain application ignoring mandatory Artha Rin Adalat Ain provisions; parties cannot bypass statutory remedies

respondent: Government of Bangladesh, Ministry of Primary and Mass Education and others
facts_summary: Nationalized primary school teachers challenged rules under the 2013 Service Conditions Rules that reduced their effective service years by 50% and created discriminatory seniority provisions favouring direct recruits over nationalized teachers, affecting their promotions and time scales.
statutes_cited: অধিগ্রহণকৃত বেসরকারি প্রাথমিক বিশ্ববিদ্যালয়ের শিক্ষক (চাকুরি শর্তাবলী নির্ধারণ) বিধিমালা ২০১৩ Rules 2(Ga) and 9(1)
notes: Rule 9(1) of 2013 Rules struck down as manifestly unreasonable and self-contradictory; nationalized teachers' seniority rights protected

facts_summary: Candidate who successfully passed all four stages of BCS examination was recommended by the Bangladesh Public Service Commission but the appropriate government agency refused appointment without showing any valid or lawful reason.
statutes_cited: Bangladesh Civil Service Recruitment Rules 1981 Rule 4(3)(A)(B); Constitution
notes: Refusing appointment of BPSC-recommended candidate without valid cause declared unlawful and unconstitutional

respondent: Secretary, Ministry of Civil Aviation, Bangladesh and others
facts_summary: Biman Bangladesh Airlines employee challenged promotion decisions that were based solely on interview performance, disregarding accumulated ACR service records over years. This practice created scope for arbitrariness and unlawful decisions.
statutes_cited: Service promotion rules; ACR-based evaluation principles
notes: Promotion solely by interview found unfair; petitioner deprived unlawfully and deserves retrospective promotion

facts_summary: Government school and college teachers challenged Nitimala 2012 prohibiting engagement in coaching business, arguing it was unconstitutional and that the Anti-Corruption Commission had no jurisdiction to inquire into their coaching activities.
statutes_cited: Public Servant (Discipline and Appeal) Rules 1985; Penal Code 1860 Section 166; Anti-Corruption Commission Act 2004; Nitimala 2012
notes: Nitimala upheld as valid supplement to existing service rules; teachers already prohibited from coaching by their service rules

respondent: Government of Bangladesh, Ministry of Post Telecommunications and IT and others
facts_summary: Government employees employed temporarily for 10-15 years challenged an executive order amendment affecting their regularization. The authority had extended their employment consistently over many years, creating a legitimate expectation of permanent absorption.
statutes_cited: Legitimate expectation doctrine; Constitutional rights
notes: Employees' legitimate expectation of absorption upheld after years of continuous service; executive order amendment found whimsical and beyond authority

facts_summary: Government temporary contingent staff who had served for 10-15 years sought regularization of their posts. The authority had been satisfied with their work and recommended regularization but failed to act on the recommendation.
statutes_cited: Legitimate expectation doctrine; service regularization principles
notes: Long-serving temporary government employees cannot be denied expectation of regularization especially when their work was satisfactory

facts_summary: Doctor petitioner lost seniority despite making application for promotion before a colleague who was subsequently promoted. The authority failed to process petitioner's application in time, resulting in unjust loss of seniority through no fault of the petitioner.
statutes_cited: Service promotion rules; principles of natural justice and equity
notes: Seniority ordered to be restored from date of colleague's promotion; writ court as court of equity cannot allow unjust loss of seniority

appellant: Md. Yousuf Ali Akon and others
facts_summary: Petitioners were appointed as Master Pilots on probation with an express promise in the BIWTA advertisement that they would be permanently absorbed after satisfactory completion of one year probation. BIWTA failed to absorb them despite satisfactory service.
statutes_cited: Service regulations; legitimate expectation doctrine
notes: Petitioners had legitimate expectation of absorption based on express promise in advertisement; BIWTA directed to permanently absorb them

facts_summary: A Steno-Typist was promoted to Stenographer and claimed entitlement to a fresh slot of time scales after promotion. The question was whether benefits drawn as Steno-Typist prevented entitlement to time scales in the promoted post of Stenographer.
statutes_cited: Service rules; time scale regulations; promotion benefits
notes: Promoted Stenographer entitled to fresh time scales in new post; previous time scales as Steno-Typist do not bar entitlement in promoted post

facts_summary: Case clarifying the meaning of a person in the service of the Republic for Administrative Tribunal jurisdiction, specifically whether the definition includes persons who have been retired, dismissed, removed, or discharged from service.
statutes_cited: Administrative Tribunal Act; Constitution
notes: Retired, dismissed and discharged government servants fall within Administrative Tribunal jurisdiction for service-related grievances

appellant: Gazi A.K.M. Fazlul Haque and others
facts_summary: Privatization Commission officers challenged promotion criteria and process. Court examined whether seniority alone was sufficient for promotion and what role merit and selection committees played in the promotion process for Deputy Directors seeking promotion to Director.
statutes_cited: Privatization Commission Officers and Employees Service Regulations 2002; Constitution Article 102
notes: Both seniority and merit required for promotion; petitioners entitled to be considered for promotion under proper criteria including minimum 5 years service

facts_summary: Petitioners challenged disciplinary action taken against them without issuing proper show cause notice as required under service rules and without forming a three-member enquiry committee to dispose of disciplinary proceedings within 60 days as mandated.
statutes_cited: Government service disciplinary rules; show cause notice requirement Rule 5.4
notes: Disciplinary action set aside; mandatory show cause notice and enquiry committee requirements under service rules were not followed

respondent: Bangabandhu Sheikh Mujib Medical University (BSMMU) and others
facts_summary: Petitioner challenged an appointment at BSMMU alleging the selection committee abused its power in violation of Article 31 of the Constitution. The case raised whether unlawful appointments could be validated by the passage of time and rendering of service in the post.
statutes_cited: Constitution of Bangladesh Article 31; BSMMU appointment rules
notes: Court held unlawful appointments cannot be validated by service; selection committee abuse of power is ground for setting aside interview result

facts_summary: Land dispute in Chittagong Hill Tracts involving declaration of a deed and mutation case. Court examined mandatory notice requirements to statutory authorities under CHT Regulation 1900 before filing suits, and the binding nature of CHT customary laws.
statutes_cited: Chittagong Hill Tracts Regulation 1900; Registration Act 1908; Constitution Article 152
notes: Case establishes mandatory notice requirement in CHT land suits; outcome for specific parties not determinable from available ratio

facts_summary: Petitioners challenged a decision of the university Syndicate that affected their rights. Court found the Syndicate had grossly violated well-settled principles of natural justice in taking a prejudicial decision without acting fairly and in accordance with law.
statutes_cited: Principles of natural justice; administrative fairness doctrine
notes: Syndicate order vitiated as gross violation of natural justice; failure to act fairly leads to arbitrariness which vitiates the impugned order

facts_summary: Plaintiff sought permanent injunction over property where defendant claimed title based on City Corporation tax receipts without a legitimate chain of title. Court identified defendant's claim as that of a land grabber whose deeds did not refer to any specific immovable property.
statutes_cited: Code of Civil Procedure; property title and chain of ownership
notes: City Corporation tax receipt alone not proof of possession; land grabber's claim rejected as isolated from any valid chain of title

facts_summary: Petitioner challenged an order rejecting their application to dismiss a plaint on grounds of res judicata. The suit land was declared as forest by a Gazette notification in 1952 and subsequently acquired by the Government under the State Acquisition and Tenancy Act. The Forest Department later attempted additional declarations which the Court found unnecessary.
statutes_cited: Forest Act 1927; State Acquisition and Tenancy Act Sections 3(2) and 20(2a)(iii)
notes: Rule discharged; Gazette notification declaring land as forest sufficient to determine character without additional Forest Act declaration

facts_summary: International commodity trading company sought appointment of an arbitrator from the respondent Vietnamese state corporation for formation of an arbitration tribunal. The respondent denied the existence of any arbitration agreement. Parties communicated through a broker whose status was disputed. Court examined whether email communications created contractual obligations.
statutes_cited: Arbitration Act 2001 Sections 9, 12, 17
notes: Rule discharged; no arbitration agreement found between parties; email communications did not create contractual obligation

facts_summary: Petitioner challenged an interlocutory order passed in an Arbitration Miscellaneous Case rejecting their application to call for records of arbitration proceedings from the arbitrators. Key question was whether civil revision is maintainable against such interlocutory orders under the Arbitration Act 2001.
statutes_cited: Arbitration Act 2001 Section 42; Code of Civil Procedure Section 115
notes: Rule discharged; civil revision maintainable but the application was unnecessary as petitioner could obtain records from his own arbitrator

appellant: Prof. Dr. Muhammad Yunus and others
facts_summary: Petitioners challenged charge framing order against them under the Bangladesh Labour Act 2006 at Grameen Telecom. Labour Inspector found violations including failure to make employees permanent after probation, failure to grant annual leave with pay, and failure to constitute Labour Participation Fund and Labour Welfare Fund.
statutes_cited: Bangladesh Labour Act 2006 Sections 303(Uma), 307, 117, 314; Labour Welfare Foundation Law 2006
notes: Rule discharged; charge framing order upheld; limitation question is mixed question of law and fact requiring evidence; nonprofit company still liable for labor welfare contributions

facts_summary: Pre-emptors filed pre-emption case claiming land was sold behind their back. Evidence showed that the vendors had approached the pre-emptors before the sale to sell the land but the pre-emptors refused and advised the sale to the current pre-emptee. This conduct constituted waiver of pre-emption rights.
statutes_cited: State Acquisition and Tenancy Act 1950 Section 96; Registration Act 1908 Section 60; Non-Agricultural Tenancy Act 1949 Section 24
notes: Rule discharged; pre-emptors waived pre-emption right through their conduct before and after the sale

respondent: Judge, Artha Rin Adalat, Chattogram and others
facts_summary: Petitioner guarantor filed writ petition against a civil detention order issued in an Artha Rin execution case without the bank filing the application properly under Section 34. Court distinguished between accused persons and judgment debtors, holding that judgment debtors need not surrender to court like criminal accused.
statutes_cited: Artha Rin Adalat Ain 2003 Sections 2, 4, 5, 6(1), 6(5), 34, 41, 44
notes: Rule made absolute; civil detention order quashed as bank did not file application under Section 34 with proper verification or affidavit

facts_summary: Chattogram Port Authority challenged a suit filed against it for land dispute without service of prior notice under Section 49 of the Chittagong Port Ordinance 1976. Port Authority argued the suit was not maintainable for non-service of notice. Court held that after joining issue, plaint cannot be rejected and service of summons satisfied the notice requirement.
statutes_cited: Chittagong Port Ordinance 1976 Section 49; Code of Civil Procedure Order VII Rule 2
notes: Rule discharged; Port Authority's challenge failed; service of summons with plaint equivalent to required notice where no alternative remedy exists for land disputes

facts_summary: Petitioner challenged lower court order condoning 188-day delay in filing an application to set aside an arbitral award under Section 42 of the Arbitration Act 2001. Question was whether Section 5 of the Limitation Act applies to condone delay in arbitral award challenge proceedings.
statutes_cited: Arbitration Act 2001 Section 42; Limitation Act 1908 Sections 5 and 29
notes: Rule made absolute; Section 5 of Limitation Act not applicable to special law with specific limitation period; lower court order condoning delay reversed

facts_summary: Plaintiffs filed suit for declaration that a registered sale deed was forged, arguing the document was not read out to illiterate parties before execution. Trial court decreed the suit but appellate court reversed. High Court found plaintiffs failed to discharge their burden of proof to rebut the presumption of valid registration.
statutes_cited: Registration Act 1908 Sections 31, 32, 34, 35; Evidence Act 1872 Sections 101, 103; Specific Relief Act Section 42
notes: Rule discharged; registered deed carries strong presumptive evidence of valid execution; plaintiff failed to rebut presumption

facts_summary: Biman Bangladesh Airlines employee challenged compulsory retirement ordered by the CEO/Managing Director without any board of directors resolution. The Articles of Association required a board resolution for retirement decisions. No delegated authority existed for the CEO to take this decision independently.
statutes_cited: Bangladesh Biman Corporation Employee Regulations 1988; Bangladesh Biman Corporation Ordinance 1977; Articles of Association
notes: Rule found merit; CEO's retirement order declared arbitrary and without jurisdiction for lack of board resolution and delegated authority

facts_summary: Petitioner company sought injunction under the Arbitration Act 2001 restraining disposal of property pending arbitration. Respondent denied existence of arbitration agreement and applied for handwriting expert examination of disputed signatures. Lower court allowed the handwriting expert application.
statutes_cited: Arbitration Act 2001 Sections 7A, 10, 17(ka), 19, 32; Evidence Act 1872 Section 45
notes: Rule made absolute; lower court exceeded limited powers under Section 7K; power to examine handwriting expert vested only in arbitral tribunal under Section 17(ka)

appellant: Probir Kumar Dey alias Saiful and another
facts_summary: Partition suit over 4.81 decimals of land after owner's death. Hindu widow claimed same share as son under the Hindu Women's Rights to Property Act 1937. One son converted to Islam before suit was filed. Court examined rights of widow, converted son, and unmarried daughter to inheritance and partition.
statutes_cited: Hindu Women's Rights to Property Act 1937 Section 3; Caste Disability Removal Act 1850; Dayabhaga school of Hindu Law
notes: Plaintiffs entitled to partition; widow has same rights as son; converted son lost right to father's property; unmarried daughter entitled to property

facts_summary: Prof. Yunus and GTC challenged charge framing order in Labour Court for alleged violations of Bangladesh Labour Act 2006 including not making probationary employees permanent, not granting annual leave, and not constituting Labour Welfare Fund. Petitioner argued as Nobel laureate he had no role in financial management.
statutes_cited: Bangladesh Labour Act 2006 Sections 303(Uma), 307, 117, 314; Labour Welfare Foundation Law 2006
notes: Rule discharged; charge framing upheld; Board of Directors responsible for GTC administration; nonprofit company not exempt from Labour Welfare Fund contribution

facts_summary: Certificate debtor challenged certificate case proceedings initiated for a defaulted loan. The Certificate Officer started proceedings without proper objective assessment of whether the outstanding dues claimed by the bank were actually payable, acting only on an improperly filed requisition letter.
statutes_cited: Public Demands Recovery Act 1913 Sections 4, 6, 16; Artha Rin Adalat Ain 2003 Section 5(5)
notes: Certificate proceeding quashed; Certificate Officer acted without objective satisfaction about actual outstanding dues

facts_summary: Associate Professor of Dhaka University challenged demotion to Assistant Professor by University Syndicate for alleged plagiarism. The Syndicate punished her without following mandatory procedures — no formal charge was framed, no copy of enquiry report was provided, and no meaningful opportunity to defend was given.
statutes_cited: Enquiry Committee and Tribunal Regulations 1980 Regulation 7(a); Dhaka University Order 1973 Article 52; First Statutes of Dhaka University
notes: Syndicate decision declared illegal; mandatory charge framing and opportunity to be heard not followed; decision vitiated by bias and malafide

facts_summary: Plaintiffs filed title suit alleging forged documents, claiming their father was bedridden, blind, and incapacitated from 1980 until death and could not have signed the disputed deeds. Defendants denied this. Trial court decreed for plaintiffs; appellate court reversed.
statutes_cited: Evidence Act 1872 Sections 101, 103; Registration Act 1908; Limitation Act 1908 Article 120
notes: Suit set aside; plaintiff failed to discharge burden of proof; suit also barred by limitation as cause of action arose earlier than claimed

facts_summary: Former Additional District Judge challenged Rule 300(a) of Bangladesh Service Rules Part I which forfeited pension upon resignation from service. Petitioner had nineteen years of unblemished service and applied for pension after resignation, which was denied by the Comptroller and Auditor General.
statutes_cited: Bangladesh Service Rules Part I Rule 300; Constitution of Bangladesh Articles 27, 31
notes: Rule 300(a) on pension forfeiture upon resignation declared ultra vires the Constitution; person who resigns is entitled to pension based on length of service

facts_summary: Joint venture petitioner was declared final responsive tenderer for Bangladesh Railway online ticketing system. A competitor filed a complaint before the Review Panel alleging evaluation irregularities. Review Panel allowed the complaint and recommended re-tender. Petitioner challenged the decision as the complaint was filed out of time.
statutes_cited: Public Procurement Act 2006 Sections 29, 30; Public Procurement Rules 2008 Rules 56, 57, 60
notes: Review Panel decision set aside; respondent's complaint was barred by limitation as filed beyond the 7-day period; Review Panel failed to address limitation

facts_summary: Suit for cancellation of a Nadabinama deed. Plaintiffs claimed the deed was not read out to them before their signatures were obtained, and they were misled into believing it was a partition deed. The defendants are literate and plaintiffs are illiterate rural people.
statutes_cited: Evidence Act 1872 Section 102; Limitation Act 1908 Section 18; Registration Act 1908
notes: Rule discharged upholding deed cancellation; reading out a deed to illiterate executants before execution is a custom having force of law

facts_summary: Auction purchasers of RAJUK land obtained title through court execution proceedings confirmed by Appellate Division. ACC then issued notices against them alleging evasion of registration fees. Petitioners challenged the notices as mala fide since the auction purchase was judicially confirmed.
statutes_cited: Anti-Corruption Commission Act 2004 Sections 19, 20; ACC Rules 2007 Rules 3(5) and 20; Registration Act 1908 Section 63A
notes: ACC notices quashed; evasion of registration fees for court-confirmed auction sale does not constitute scheduled offence under ACC Act; notices issued mala fide

facts_summary: Managing Director and shareholder of a Borrower-Company challenged Bangladesh Bank notices threatening to vacate his directorship at Mercantile Bank due to failure to repay rescheduled loan down payment as guarantor of the Borrower-Company.
statutes_cited: Banking Companies Act 1991 Section 17(8); Companies Act 1994 Section 43; Artha Rin Adalat Ain 2003 Sections 3, 5, 6
notes: Petition dismissed; directorship vacates when director is defaulter guarantor; distinction between defaulter loanee and defaulter guarantor does not protect directorship
